nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in slow-moving to moderately-flowing freshwater rivers, streams, lakes, and reservoirs with soft sand or fine-gravel beds. Partially buries into the substrate with its short siphons protruding to filter particulate matter, microalgae and bacteria from the water column. Tolerates a wide thermal and chemical range in the wild and has spread far beyond its native range as a result.

decorPreferences: fine sand or very fine gravel substrate at least 5 cm deep for partial burrowing
mature, well-established tank (ideally 6+ months) with stable biofilm and microalgae populations
moderate water flow from a filter outlet or small powerhead to deliver suspended food across the bed
open sand bed without sharp décor or stones that can chip the shell
avoid copper-containing plumbing, medications and fertilisers

diet.notes: An obligate filter feeder. Has been documented straining suspended phytoplankton, bacteria, fine detritus and dissolved organic particles from the water column at very high filtration rates (commonly reported around 1 L per hour per clam). Sparsely-stocked or heavily-filtered tanks tend to starve them; supplemental dosing of liquid phytoplankton, very fine powdered foods (spirulina powder, Golden Pearls 5–50 µm, rotifer feeds) and a mature biofilm are generally advised to keep them alive long-term.

goodWith: small peaceful community fish (tetras, rasboras, small barbs)
avoidWith: any tankmate frequently treated with copper-based medications

imageCandidates.notes: Holger Krisp; 4000x2666. Two glossy live clams with vibrant golden-tan shells, dark umbones and crisp concentric growth ridges — tack sharp and well lit, best showing the species' diagnostic golden coloration.
Smithsonian Environmental Research Center; 1832x1584. Clean single specimen on dark background, golden-yellow with brown mottling and sharply defined concentric ridges; whole shell in frame.
Franco Folini; 2027x1520. Natural in-situ pair on a river substrate, golden-tan shells with visible growth rings; sharp, adds habitat variety (slightly harsh daylight).

summary: The Golden Clam (Corbicula fluminea) is a freshwater bivalve from East Asia sold under trade names including Asian Gold Clam, Good Luck Clam, and Prosperity Clam. Adults reach 2.5–6.5 cm. An obligate filter feeder, it generally requires a mature tank with a fine sand bed. It is invasive in many regions and must never be released.
careNotes: The Golden Clam is the most commonly encountered freshwater bivalve in the aquarium trade and is sold under a range of trade names — Asian Gold Clam, Asian Clam, Good Luck Clam, Freshwater Golden Clam, Prosperity Clam — that all refer to the same species, Corbicula fluminea. It is widely advertised as easy because it filters water and looks ornamental, but in practice it is generally considered an advanced-level animal: it is an obligate filter feeder with no way to chase down food, and most aquariums simply do not carry enough suspended phytoplankton, bacteria and fine detritus to keep one alive for more than a few months without active supplementation. Care guides commonly recommend dosing liquid phytoplankton, spirulina powder or very fine particulate foods (e.g. Golden Pearls 5–50 µm) several times a week, and keeping the clam in a mature tank (often suggested at 6+ months old) with a soft sand bed at least 5 cm deep into which it partially buries. Moderate flow across the bed helps deliver food. Like all freshwater inverts it is highly copper-sensitive — copper-based medications, fertilisers and even older copper plumbing should be avoided. Dead clams decompose extremely fast and can crash water quality, so a clam that stops responding to gentle touch and remains gaping should be removed immediately. The species is hermaphroditic and has been reported to reproduce in aquaria in rare cases, broadcasting large numbers of pediveliger larvae; this is part of why it is regarded as one of the worst freshwater invasive species worldwide. Trade and release rules differ by jurisdiction — some US states, Canadian provinces and EU member states explicitly prohibit possession, transport or release. Even where keeping is legal, this species should NEVER be released to the wild or flushed; kept stock should be euthanised humanely if no longer wanted.
breedingNotes: Corbicula fluminea is a self-fertilising hermaphrodite and has occasionally been reported to release pediveliger larvae directly into aquarium water; sustained reproduction in home tanks is uncommon and not normally a hobby goal. Any successful broadcast is a biosecurity concern given the species' invasive status — larvae should not be allowed to reach natural waterways via drained tank water or shared equipment.

additional.notes: Designated slice primary (Aquarium Co-Op) returned zero search results for 'corbicula', 'asian clam' or 'gold clam' — no dedicated species page exists. Role swapped: aquariumbreeder.com promoted to primary, this URL listed only for traceability.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated slice secondary (Practical Fishkeeping) returned HTTP 403 on the search endpoint and no usable care page could be reached — substituted with Aquariadise and Aquarium Care Basics for cross-verification.

additional.name: Aquariadise — Freshwater Clams Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (~7.6 cm), temperature (18–29 °C), pH (6.5–8.0), kH (5–12), lifespan (6 months–2 years), 'advanced' care rating, fine sand 5–7 cm deep, hermaphroditic reproduction and invasive status.

additional.name: Aquarium Care Basics — Asian Gold Clam
additional.notes: Cross-checked stock size 3–3.5 in (7.6–9 cm), temperature 21–29 °C, pH 7.0–8.0, hard water preference, copper sensitivity, ~29 gal (~110 L) recommendation and tankmate guidance. Lifespan 'about a year, sometimes 3+'.

additional.name: USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species — Corbicula fluminea
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Corbicula fluminea O. F. Müller 1774, Cyrenidae, Venerida), maximum shell length (<50 mm reported), native range across temperate-to-tropical southern Asia and invasive status in the US.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Corbicula fluminea is one of the world's most damaging freshwater invasive species and is established across the US, much of mainland Europe, the UK and Ireland. Possession, transport or release is restricted or prohibited in a number of US states, Canadian provinces and EU member states — keepers must check local law before purchase and must never release stock to natural waterways.
